title: Vegetarian shepherd's pie
subtitle: Green lentil and mushroom base, mashed potato top
excerpt: Puy lentils and chestnut mushrooms in a rich tomato and red wine sauce, topped with buttery mashed potato. The vegetarian version that earns its name rather than just borrowing it.
sourceNotes: Recipe synthesised from the British vegetarian-cooking tradition of the 1980s onwards, when lentil-based meat alternatives became common in home cooking. Puy lentils as a protein base for baked pies is documented in Rose Elliot's Vegetarian Cookery (1988) and subsequent British vegetarian recipe collections.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before or after baking for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight and reheat covered at 180°C for 25 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily and freezes well. Make in two dishes, eat one and freeze one.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 2 days ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Add 10 minutes to the baking time from cold.

content.text: The success of a vegetarian shepherd's pie depends on one thing: the filling must have as much depth as the lamb version, not simply be a lighter, thinner proxy. Puy lentils provide texture and body; dried mushrooms (if you have them) provide the umami that carries the flavour; a proper red wine reduction gives the sauce its backbone. Skip any of these elements and the result tastes thin and virtuous rather than satisfying.

Make the mash generous and well-seasoned. Mash that is too stiff will crack and shrink in the oven; mash that is properly buttery and loose will stay in contact with the filling and brown softly on top.

items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml boiling water for 20 min
Maris Piper, about 1 kg, peeled and quartered

content.text: Soak the dried porcini if using. Heat a little olive oil in a Dutch oven over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion and {{carrot}} diced carrots for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the chestnut mushrooms and cook over high heat for 5 minutes until their liquid has evaporated. Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for 1 minute.

Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock, {{puy-lentils}} of rinsed Puy lentils, the thyme sprigs, and the chopped rosemary. If using porcini, add them with their soaking liquid (leaving the last gritty tablespoon behind). Season well and cook, partially covered, for 25 to 30 minutes until the lentils are tender and the sauce has thickened. Remove the thyme. Taste and adjust.

Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Boil {{potato}} potato quarters in salted water for 20 minutes until tender. Drain well and leave to steam-dry for 2 minutes. Mash thoroughly, then beat in {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter and {{whole-milk}} of warmed milk until smooth and creamy. Season generously.

Spoon the lentil filling into a baking dish and spread the mash evenly over the top, covering all the way to the edges to prevent the filling from bubbling out around the sides. Rough up the surface with a fork to create ridges that will crisp. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden brown. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Filling is too liquid under the mash
items.cause: Lentils were not cooked with the lid off long enough to reduce the sauce
items.fix: Cook the filling uncovered for another 10 minutes before adding the mash. It should be thick enough to hold a spoon shape.

items.cause: Mash was too stiff and dry
items.fix: Beat in a little more warm milk before spreading. The mash should be loose enough to spread easily but firm enough to hold the fork ridges.

items.symptom: Lentils are still firm after 30 minutes
items.cause: Acid from the tomatoes inhibited the lentils cooking through
items.fix: Do not add the tinned tomatoes until the lentils have had a 10-minute head start in the stock alone.

items.cause: Dried porcini not used, or Worcestershire sauce omitted
items.fix: Add a tablespoon of dark soy sauce and a teaspoon of Marmite to the filling for umami depth.

content.text: The vegetarian shepherd's pie emerged as a distinct recipe rather than a substitution during the 1980s, when British vegetarianism moved out of health-food shops and into the mainstream. Lentils provided the structure that minced meat had given the original; the challenge was always achieving the same depth of flavour without the fat content of lamb mince. The key, identified by vegetarian cooks including Rose Elliot, was a long braise in red wine with dried mushrooms — the same technique that makes a good French daube, applied to pulses.
